topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee shop hummed with the low murmur of conversation, a sound that usually brought a comfortable warmth to me. Today, it felt like a spotlight. Liam was laughing, a bright, easy sound that always used to signal our shared history, but when he turned to order another latte, his eyes swept over the room, and they paused—not on me, but just past me, as if confirming something that wasn't there. My hands tightened around my mug until my knuckles were white. I watched him, a knot forming tight in my chest, a physical pressure that made breathing shallow. I desperately wanted to melt into the worn upholstery, to become invisible. When he finally turned back, his expression was polite, distant, utterly devoid of the familiar spark I remembered. I mumbled an apology for taking up space and fled to a secluded corner table, the taste of bitter coffee suddenly overwhelming.

[story 2]

I kept catching my reflection in the darkened window of the library. My face looked strained, pulled tight around the edges. It wasn't the silence of the vast room that was the problem; it was the echoing absence of a shared memory. I replayed the last few weeks in my head—the tentative texts, the inside jokes, the easy camaraderie that had defined my adolescence. Now, standing in the hallway, I saw him—my oldest friend—and the casual ease of our connection was gone, replaced by a thick, uncrossable pane of glass. I managed a small, jerky nod when he approached, my voice coming out thin and reedy. "Hey," I managed, the sound feeling foreign in my own ears. He offered a quick, practiced smile, a gesture that didn't reach his eyes, and then he moved on. I felt a hot flush creep up my neck, a desperate need to disappear into the floorboards beneath my feet.

[story 3]

The reunion party was a suffocating affair, filled with forced enthusiasm and superficial chatter. I stood near the edge of the crowd, nursing a glass of sparkling water, trying to appear engaged, trying to look like I belonged. Every time Liam laughed with someone else, a sharp, internal clench seized my stomach. I watched him interact, the easy familiarity we once shared now a phantom limb, aching where something vital used to be. When he finally glanced my way across the room, his gaze was fleeting, a quick inventory, and then it was gone. The realization hit me like a physical blow: he simply didn't register me. My shoulders slumped involuntarily, a sudden weight settling on my back. I gripped my glass so hard the condensation threatened to spill over, wishing the floor would open up and swallow me whole.
